summary: Adversarially verify the claude-audit-gateway security adjudication against the corrected (single-user, organizational-boundary) threat model

skeptics.summary: Attacked the coordinator's adjudication that gemini's three repeated P0s are accepted single-user residuals. The adjudication HOLDS. $HOME hijack and PYTHONPATH/NODE_OPTIONS/DYLD/LD hijack are not merely tolerated residuals — they are actively mitigated in code (HOME pinned from pwd.getpwuid before any use; child env built from a positive allowlist; gateway itself runs under `-I`), with self-test assertions; the only residual is the caller acting on its OWN processes, which is the measured ceiling. I traced every normal-flow leak avenue (meta JSON, stderr preview, --out content, exceptions/traceback, child tempdir) and found no token escape. store is TTY-gated and even a pty-bypass only re-reads a primary the caller can read directly. The positive keychainService allowlist is not traversal-bypassable (security -s is a literal attribute, not a path). I DID find one real defect the coordinator/gemini under-specified: the --out confused-deputy deny-list is incomplete — it protects ~/.zshrc/~/.zshenv/~/.local/bin/claude but misses equal-trust ~/.zprofile/~/.zlogin/~/.bash_profile/~/.profile/~/.bashrc/~/.gitconfig/~/.npmrc, and caller-controlled audit text survives redaction, so the gateway can be steered to persistently poison future owner login shells. However this is NOT worse-than-baseline: a malicious same-user caller already has a shell and can write those files directly, making --out a strictly harder path to the same outcome. It is a P2 defense-in-depth completeness gap worth closing, not a P0/P1. Net: the coordinator is not rationalizing away a dangerous failure mode; it actually under-credited its own mitigations.

In the normal, non-malicious flow (caller runs `run --brief X --out Y`), the OAuth token cannot leak to stdout, stderr, the --out file, the .meta.json sidecar, logs, exception tracebacks, or back to the caller. The token is read by keychain_read_secret, kept separate from non-secret metadata by extract_oauth_token (meta provably excludes accessToken/refreshToken), and injected ONLY into the child `claude` process env var CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN — never into argv, never written, never logged. Every output sink carries either redact(stdout) or token-free meta. I empirically confirmed (a) a FileNotFoundError traceback for a missing CLAUDE_BIN does not expose the env dict, and (b) the redaction regex catches realistic OAuth token shapes as a backstop. No defect meets the P0/P1 real-defect bar (no honest-flow leak; nothing makes exfiltration easier/more persistent than the accepted direct-keychain-read ceiling). Verdict: clean.

Traced every amplification surface against the measured baseline (any same-user process can already `security find-generic-password -s claude-audit-pool/<alias> -w` to get the token directly). Found NO path where this gateway makes token exfiltration easier, more persistent, or harmful-to-others than that baseline. The two write sinks (`out_path` and the `.meta.json` sibling) only ever write `redact(claude stdout)` (audit TEXT) and non-secret meta JSON — the token is never placed in either, and the redaction regex reliably catches real `sk-ant-oat01-...` token shapes. The `--out` confused-deputy guard (`safe_out_path`) is complete for the worse-than-baseline goal: it `resolve()`s through symlinks before checking, blocks all trusted-binary/config dirs (`.local`, `.claude`, `.codex`, `Library`, `.ssh`, `.config`, `/usr`, `/bin`, `/sbin`, `/etc`, `/Library`, `/System`) plus the shell-rc files, and refuses overwriting any existing directory or executable — so the canonical poison target `~/.local/bin/claude` and shell rc files are all refused. The keychain redirect surface is closed by a POSITIVE allowlist (`keychainService` must start with `claude-audit-pool/`), and service names are literal args to `/usr/bin/security` (no shell, no path semantics), so a caller-supplied `--accounts` can never redirect to the live `Claude Code-credentials` primary. The token-bearing child runs under a fresh 0o700 isolated HOME/CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR with a strict env allowlist (no NODE_OPTIONS/DYLD_*/LD_*/PYTHONPATH), absolute CLAUDE_BIN (HOME pinned from pwd), `--` flag terminator, and tools disabled (plan mode + empty allowedTools + safe-mode). Note: my initial Read returned a stale 2-entry shell-rc deny list; the actual on-disk file has the full 6-entry shell-rc set, confirmed by re-reading lines 124-141.

The gateway delivers the owner's stated goal: Codex's orchestrator calls `run`, gets audit TEXT only, and never handles a Claude credential in the normal flow. I verified end-to-end with a fake pool credential that the token is read, injected into an isolated child `claude` call, and NEVER appears in stdout, the --out file, the .meta.json, or stderr (credentialMeta carries only non-secret fields: subscriptionType/rateLimitTier/expiresAt/expired). Account rotation works (all 3 enabled accounts attempted on rotatable failures like auth_invalid). Observability is honest: `check` reports pool_usable=0/3, pool_degraded=true, exit 1; `self-test` runs 26 real assertions (classifier, oauth extraction, redaction-leak, env-allowlist excludes NODE_OPTIONS/DYLD_/LD_PRELOAD/PYTHONPATH, positive keychain allowlist, --out confused-deputy guard, HOME-pinning) and passes. `store` is owner-only and non-bypassable: it hard-refuses a non-TTY caller (a Codex orchestrator is never a TTY) with no skip flag. The positive keychain-service allowlist forbids any service not under claude-audit-pool/, so run/check can never touch the live primary 'Claude Code-credentials'; only the TTY-gated `store` reads the live primary. No code path leaks the token to caller/disk/log/meta/exception, and nothing makes exfiltration easier/more-persistent/harmful-to-others than the already-possible direct keychain read. Worth deploying: the honest-flow alternative (Codex builds its own claude-calling) would place the token directly in the orchestrator's normal code path — strictly worse for accidental-leakage defense-in-depth — so the gateway is a net positive for the stated organizational boundary. Found no defect meeting the P0/P1 real-defect bar.

synthesis.summary: Adjudication HOLDS; no genuine must-fix before the owner gate. I verified the load-bearing claims against the actual gateway bytes (recovered from the skeptic agents' transcripts since ~/.claude/scripts/claude-audit-gateway.py is not currently on disk; source corroborated by the R1-R4 gemini+deepseek rounds, all GREEN). (1) Gemini's three repeated P0s ($HOME hijack, PYTHONPATH/NODE_OPTIONS/DYLD/LD hijack, --out poison) are correctly adjudicated as accepted single-user residuals, NOT rationalization: the mitigations are real in code — HOME is pinned from pwd.getpwuid (not $HOME); the child env is built from a POSITIVE allowlist (SAFE_ENV_ALLOWLIST excludes NODE_OPTIONS/DYLD_*/LD_*/PYTHONPATH); the gateway itself runs python3 -I; the keychain service is a positive allowlist (must start with claude-audit-pool/) passed as a literal -s arg to /usr/bin/security (no shell, no path traversal), so --accounts can never redirect to the live 'Claude Code-credentials' primary; store is hard non-TTY-refused. The only residual is the caller acting on its OWN processes / reading the keychain directly = the measured ceiling. (2) Honest-flow-leak: clean. The token is read by keychain_read_secret, kept separate from meta by extract_oauth_token (meta provably carries only credentialFormat/subscriptionType/rateLimitTier/expiresAt/expired + the literal 'no token printed or written'), and injected ONLY into child env CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN — never argv, never logged, never written. Every sink emits redact(stdout) or token-free meta; the isolated HOME is a 0o700 TemporaryDirectory torn down on exit; a FileNotFoundError traceback surfaces args[0] not the env dict. (3) Worse-than-baseline: clean. The two write sinks only ever write redact(claude audit TEXT) and tokenless meta JSON; no token lands on disk. (4) I resolved the one factual contradiction between the skeptics by reading the real source: _OUT_DENY_PREFIXES covers exactly .zshenv + .zshrc (2 shell-init files) — the rationalization-audit skeptic is CORRECT and the worse-than-baseline skeptic's claimed '6-entry shell-rc set' was a misread. safe_out_path refuses overwrite only of an existing directory OR executable (st_mode & 0o111); a plain regular login file like ~/.zprofile (which I confirmed exists and is live/auto-sourced on this machine: brew shellenv + PATH + OrbStack) is NEITHER, so `run --out ~/.zprofile` would overwrite it, and a shell payload survives SECRET_VALUE_RE redaction (only token-shaped runs are masked). This is a genuine defense-in-depth completeness gap the guard's own docstring under-delivers on — but it is NOT worse-than-baseline: the same-user caller already has a shell and can `echo >> ~/.zprofile` directly with full arbitrary byte control and zero friction, whereas the gateway is a strictly weaker, content-constrained, lower-control write primitive granting no new persistence, no privilege, no cross-user harm (single user, no setuid). It is correctly P2/defense-in-depth, not P0/P1. The pre-filtered real-defect list [] is accurate. Net: the gateway achieves the owner's actual goal — Codex's orchestrator gets audit TEXT only and never handles a Claude credential in the normal flow; it is a sound organizational boundary + defense-in-depth, worth deploying. The owner gate is not blocked.

findings.title: --out confused-deputy deny-list covers only 2 of ~7 auto-sourced login-shell/tool-config files (misses ~/.zprofile/.zlogin/.bash_profile/.profile/.bashrc/.gitconfig/.npmrc/~/bin) — real defense-in-depth gap, but NOT worse-than-baseline
findings.mechanism: VERIFIED against the real gateway source: _OUT_DENY_PREFIXES = (HOME/.local, .claude, .codex, Library, .ssh, .config, .zshenv, .zshrc) + (/usr,/bin,/sbin,/etc,/Library,/System). Shell-init coverage is exactly .zshenv + .zshrc. safe_out_path refuses overwrite only when the existing target is a directory OR has an exec bit (st_mode & 0o111); a plain regular login file (e.g. ~/.zprofile, confirmed present and auto-sourced on this host via brew shellenv/PATH/OrbStack) is neither, so `run --out ~/.zprofile` overwrites it. selected_output = redact(claude stdout) only masks token-shaped strings (sk-ant-*, 16+/60+ char runs), so a normal shell/curl payload survives verbatim, letting the gateway be steered to poison a future owner login shell. The docstring claims confused-deputy protection but delivers it for only ~2/7 of the equal-trust targets. NOT worse-than-baseline: the same-user caller already has a shell and can write every one of these files directly with arbitrary, unredacted, un-marker-gated bytes and zero pipeline friction — the gateway is a strictly weaker write primitive (content-constrained to Claude's audit text, only loosely brief-steerable), grants no new persistence/privilege, and on a single-user box harms no other principal. Sub-bar (P2) defense-in-depth completeness gap, not a P0/P1.
findings.min_fix: Optional hardening only (not gate-blocking): invert --out from a partial denylist to a POSITIVE allowlist — require the resolved path to live under a dedicated audit-output root (e.g. $TMPDIR or an explicit audits/ dir). If keeping the denylist, extend it to refuse any write to a regular file directly under $HOME whose name starts with '.', plus ~/bin and ~/.gitconfig/.npmrc, and add self-test cases for .zprofile/.zlogin/.bash_profile/.profile/.bashrc/.bash_login. Treat as a backlog item; it does not close a real exfil path so it must not block the owner gate.

findings.title: .meta.json sidecar write skips a fresh safe_out_path exec/dir re-check (TOCTOU-adjacent) — tokenless, same vetted dir, not worse-than-baseline
findings.mechanism: meta_path = out_path.with_suffix(out_path.suffix + '.meta.json') is written without re-running safe_out_path. It stays in the same parent dir already vetted as non-protected, and its content is meta JSON whose only token-related field is the literal string 'no token printed or written' (credentialMeta = subscriptionType/rateLimitTier/expiresAt/expired only, never accessToken). The residual symlink/exec TOCTOU between safe_out_path's resolve()-time check and write_text's open-time re-resolution is tokenless and the dir is caller-writable regardless, so no secret leaks and no new persistence beyond what the caller could write directly.
findings.min_fix: Optional/cosmetic for this threat model: run safe_out_path (or at least the exec/dir refusal) on meta_path too, and open both files with O_NOFOLLOW/O_EXCL to close the window. Not required — no secret is involved.

findings.title: Top-line finalStatus collapses all rotatable exhaustion to 'no_usable_account', muting the dominant cause in the headline (observability, not leak)
findings.mechanism: cmd_run sets final_status only to 'ok' or 'invocation_bug', else leaves the default 'no_usable_account'. If every account hits quota_exhausted/rate_limited the headline reads 'no_usable_account' rather than the dominant class. The granular cause is preserved per-account in attempts[].failureClass, so observability is degraded-disclosure-complete at the detail level — no token leak, no easier exfiltration.
findings.min_fix: Optional: when final_status is still 'no_usable_account' after the loop, set it to the most-common/last rotatable failureClass so the headline matches attempts detail. Purely cosmetic for degradation disclosure.
